title: The Synergistic Route to Stretched Criticality
authors: Lorenzo Lucarini, Sandro Meloni, Pablo Villegas
abstract: Griffiths phases are typically associated with quenched disorder, while frustration gives rise to multistability and spin-glass behavior. Whether extended criticality can arise in other contexts remains an open question. Here, we show that synergistic interactions provide a distinct route to non-conventional critical phenomena. By combining spreading mechanisms that reinforce activity through complementary pathways, we uncover a broad distribution of relaxation rates, leading to Griffiths-like slow dynamics and extended criticality. We demonstrate that this mechanism is robust across networks and emerges both in systems with explicit higher-order interactions and in purely pairwise systems with nonlinear dynamics.
summary: The paper shows that synergistic interactions can create a new pathway to extended criticality and Griffiths-like slow dynamics. This mechanism works by using complementary pathways to reinforce activity, and it is robust across both higher-order and nonlinear pairwise networks.
main_problem: Investigating whether extended criticality and Griffiths-like slow dynamics can arise from mechanisms other than quenched disorder or frustration.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that synergistic interactions, which reinforce activity through complementary pathways, provide a new route to non-conventional critical phenomena and a broad distribution of relaxation rates.
method: Theoretical analysis of spreading mechanisms and nonlinear dynamics within network-based models.
model_or_system: The study considers networks featuring synergistic interactions, including systems with explicit higher-order interactions and purely pairwise systems with nonlinear dynamics.
key_observables: Relaxation rates and distribution of activity/spreading dynamics.
important_parameters: Synergistic interaction strength and network topology.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of synergistic interactions, demonstrates the emergence of Griffiths-like dynamics, and shows the robustness of this mechanism across different network types and interaction orders.
why_interesting: This is interesting for many-body dynamics as it identifies a novel mechanism for achieving slow, non-exponential relaxation and extended criticality without relying on traditional quenched disorder.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
